formatted_human_prompt:
Verify this ER diagram schema:
ORIGINAL REQUIREMENT:
ER Modeling Scenario
Integrated Hospital Management System for Multiple Facilities
Business Context
You have been hired to model the database for a hospital network that operates 15 units across 3 Brazilian states. The system must control, record and monitor the entry and exit of all people who access the hospitals, including staff, visitors and patients, ensuring traceability, security and compliance with the institution's internal rules.
Functional Requirements
1. Hospital
Each hospital has departments responsible for specific activities. Not every facility in the network needs to offer all services: each hospital declares the departments, human resources and capacities it has (available teams and departments).
Departments present in the network:
* Emergency
* ICU (Intensive Care Unit)
* Surgical Center / Operating Theaters
* Adult Ward
* Pediatric Ward
* Maternity
* Radiology / Imaging (X-ray, CT, MRI)
* Clinical Laboratory
* Outpatient Clinic
* Hospital Pharmacy
* Social Services
* Triage
* Reception
1.1 Departments
* Each department has an assigned coordinator.
* There must be a leader or person responsible for the department in every shift (there should always be someone accountable for the department at any time).

2. People Management
The hospital receives individuals in multiple categories; some are employees and others are not. Categories include: visitor, patient, physician, nurse, resident physician, researcher and administrative staff. Physicians may work in multiple units under different contract types (for example, CLT, PJ, on-call, daily-rate). Residents are licensed physicians in training who also attend patients under supervision, so it is important to record the supervising physician for each resident. VIP patients have special care rules and enhanced privacy (these are procedural and privacy measures, not related to the medical quality of care). Employees may have dependents who are also patients. The hospital records the following for every person who accesses it: full name, addresses, contact phone numbers, CPF, RG and date of birth. (CPF and RG are Brazilian identifiers: CPF = taxpayer ID, RG = identity card.)
2.1 Patients
   * Each patient receives an identification wristband.
   * The system must record patient movements between departments, including hospital admission, transfers between departments and discharge.
   * A complete history of the departments each patient has passed through must be maintained.
   * The system must indicate whether the patient is awaiting care, in care, admitted (inpatient), under observation, in surgery, or discharged.
2.2 Visitors
   * Not every visitor is accompanying a patient.
   * The type of visitor must be recorded (for example, technical visit, regulatory inspection, etc.).
   * Visitor classification is essential to determine access restrictions (important information for the security team).
   * Each visitor receives an identification card at entry.
   * Not every patient is allowed to have a companion. Valid companion cases include: minors; elderly patients; pregnant patients; women in labor; postpartum patients; patients with prolonged or severe hospitalization, at the discretion of the attending physician or care team; and situations of emotional or cognitive vulnerability when the physician or social services deem it necessary.
   * If patient isolation is required, the physician must document the reason for isolation in a clinical note.
   * The system must record the visitor's exit (mandatory).
   * Access outside visiting hours must be prevented unless special authorization is granted by the hospital.
2.3 Employees
   * Employees must be clearly identified.
   * A researcher can be either a nurse or a physician only; researcher is not an independent occupational category.
   * A resident physician cannot be a coordinator or be responsible for a department, even temporarily.
   * Physicians and nurses may also hold administrative roles (for example, responsible for coordinating a department).
   * Any employee may still be treated by the hospital, be present as a visitor, or accompany a patient.
2.4 Addresses
   * A person may have more than one registered address.
   * Employees update their addresses periodically.
   * When possible, patients and visitors should also have updated addresses.
   * It is important to store the timestamp of the last address update.
   * If the last update was more than six months ago, the system should alert and request a registration update.
2.5 Identification Cards
   * Identification cards have an issue date and an expiry date, recorded in the system for security purposes.
   * Cards have a type that designates access level (visually distinguished by different colors).
   * When a card is in use it is marked as active.
   * Because patients and visitors do not have personalized cards, a card's identifier number may be temporarily linked to the person's record while in use.
   * Only personalized cards (employees) must include a photo.
3. Employee Scheduling
Hospital units require an integrated time and attendance system that covers all 15 units to prevent staff from being accidentally scheduled at two locations at the same time or assigned impractical schedules. Professionals follow different work routines. Administration must precisely control the hours worked by each employee. Different hospitals in the network may have different scheduling contracts with the same employee. It is also necessary to record events that remove an employee from duties, such as days off, medical leave, vacation or training.
3.1 Time Clock Records
   * The system must record each employee's clock-in and clock-out times.
   * As mentioned, each employee has an identification card, which facilitates this task.
   * Time records may generate an alert if an employee exceeds scheduled hours or misses a clock record; this is useful for HR.
3.2 Work Shifts
   * Some employees have more flexible shifts than others.
   * Administrative staff generally have fixed weekdays and start/end times.
   * Nurses may have fixed rules or work rotating shifts.
   * Physicians similarly may have variable schedules and may come to the hospital only to perform consultations, monitoring, surgeries, etc., leaving when those activities are completed.
   * The employment contract governs how working hours are administered by the contracting institution.
   * The key requirement is accurate recording of clock-in and clock-out times for HR verification of compliance with agreed schedules.
3.3 Availability Exceptions
   * Reasons that remove a professional from scheduled activities must be well documented.
   * Each exception should include start and end dates, type (day off, medical leave, vacation, training) and an optional textual note.
3.4 Shift Swaps
   * Shift swaps among health staff are common and permitted.
   * Whenever a swap occurs, it is important to record the involved employees and the coordinator who approved the swap.
   * Each department has its coordinator, who may be a physician, a nurse or an administrative employee.
   * The justification for the swap request should also be recorded (useful for HR metrics about the need for permanent schedule changes, employee health considerations, etc.).
4. Special Situations and Contingency Procedures
4.1 Lost Identification Card
The system must allow immediate registration of a lost card. When a loss is reported:
   * the card is automatically invalidated;
   * a new card may be issued and linked to the person;
   * every access attempt using the lost card is blocked and logged;
   * the event generates an alert for security.
A history of cards (active, replaced, lost, expired) must be maintained.
4.2 Card Failure (Defective or Inoperable Card)
When a card fails to read, the system must record: the person involved; the reason for the failure; the read attempt; and the manual verification performed (security or reception staff responsible). Manual validation of entry/exit using an official document plus employee ID/registration must be allowed. After manual validation, the system records the entry/exit with the same legal validity as an electronic read. The defective card should be marked inoperable and require replacement.
5. Reports and Queries
      * Query access history by person, date, department and type (employee, visitor, patient).
      * Generate department occupancy reports.
      * Generate reports on compliance with scheduled hours and total hours worked for a given period (month, week, day).
      * The system must alert whenever a department reaches 60%, 80% and 100% of its operational capacity.
GENERATED SCHEMA:
{'tables': {'Hospital': {'*HospitalID': 'int', 'Name': 'varchar(100)', 'State': 'varchar(50)', 'Address': 'varchar(200)'}, 'Department': {'*DepartmentID': 'int', 'Name': 'varchar(50)', 'HospitalID': 'int', 'CoordinatorID': 'int'}, 'Person': {'*PersonID': 'int', 'FullName': 'varchar(100)', 'CPF': 'varchar(14)', 'RG': 'varchar(20)', 'DOB': 'date'}, 'Patient': {'*PersonID': 'int', 'WristbandID': 'varchar(20)'}, 'Visitor': {'*PersonID': 'int', 'VisitorType': 'varchar(50)', 'CompanionStatus': 'varchar(50)', 'IsolationReason': 'text'}, 'Employee': {'*PersonID': 'int', 'Role': 'varchar(50)', 'ContractType': 'varchar(50)'}, 'Address': {'*AddressID': 'int', 'PersonID': 'int', 'AddressDetails': 'text', 'LastUpdated': 'timestamp'}, 'IdentificationCard': {'*CardID': 'int', 'PersonID': 'int', 'IssueDate': 'date', 'ExpiryDate': 'date', 'CardType': 'varchar(50)', 'ActiveStatus': 'boolean'}, 'WorkShift': {'*ShiftID': 'int', 'EmployeeID': 'int', 'StartDateTime': 'datetime', 'EndDateTime': 'datetime'}, 'TimeClockRecord': {'*RecordID': 'int', 'EmployeeID': 'int', 'ClockIn': 'datetime', 'ClockOut': 'datetime'}, 'AvailabilityException': {'*ExceptionID': 'int', 'EmployeeID': 'int', 'StartDate': 'date', 'EndDate': 'date', 'ExceptionType': 'varchar(50)', 'Notes': 'text'}, 'ShiftSwap': {'*SwapID': 'int', 'Employee1ID': 'int', 'Employee2ID': 'int', 'CoordinatorID': 'int', 'SwapDate': 'date', 'Justification': 'text'}, 'PatientMovement': {'*MovementID': 'int', 'PatientID': 'int', 'DepartmentID': 'int', 'Timestamp': 'datetime', 'Status': 'varchar(50)'}, 'DepartmentCapacityAlert': {'*AlertID': 'int', 'DepartmentID': 'int', 'AlertLevel': 'varchar(10)', 'Timestamp': 'datetime'}}, 'relations': ['Hospital:HospitalID 1--* Department:HospitalID', 'Department:DepartmentID 1--1 Employee:PersonID', 'Person:PersonID 1--1 Patient:PersonID', 'Person:PersonID 1--1 Visitor:PersonID', 'Person:PersonID 1--1 Employee:PersonID', 'Person:PersonID 1--* Address:PersonID', 'Employee:PersonID 1--* IdentificationCard:PersonID', 'Employee:PersonID 1--* WorkShift:EmployeeID', 'Employee:PersonID 1--* TimeClockRecord:EmployeeID', 'Employee:PersonID 1--* AvailabilityException:EmployeeID', 'Employee:PersonID 1--* ShiftSwap:Employee1ID', 'Employee:PersonID 1--* ShiftSwap:Employee2ID', 'Patient:PersonID 1--* PatientMovement:PatientID', 'Department:DepartmentID 1--* PatientMovement:DepartmentID', 'Department:DepartmentID 1--* DepartmentCapacityAlert:DepartmentID'], 'rankAdjustments': '', 'label': 'Integrated Hospital Management System ER Model'}

Perform the complete verification checklist (6 items).
For each item, answer the question explicitly.
List all errors with suggested fixes.
Do NOT generate a new schema. Only validate and list issues.

json_output_from_level1.label: Integrated Hospital Management System ER Model

response:
<think>
Okay, let's start by going through each of the verification checklist items one by one. 

First, the PRIMARY KEY VALIDATION. I need to check if every table has exactly one primary key marked with an asterisk. Looking at the tables provided:

- Hospital has *HospitalID – valid.
- Department has *DepartmentID – valid.
- Person has *PersonID – valid.
- Patient has *PersonID – valid.
- Visitor has *PersonID – valid.
- Employee has *PersonID – valid.
- Address has *AddressID – valid.
- IdentificationCard has *CardID – valid.
- WorkShift has *ShiftID – valid.
- TimeClockRecord has *RecordID – valid.
- AvailabilityException has *ExceptionID – valid.
- ShiftSwap has *SwapID – valid.
- PatientMovement has *MovementID – valid.
- DepartmentCapacityAlert has *AlertID – valid.

All tables have exactly one primary key. So, the answer here is YES.

Next, FOREIGN KEY VALIDATION. Each foreign key should reference a valid table and column. Let's check each foreign key in the relations:

1. Department:HospitalID references Hospital:HospitalID – valid.
2. Department:CoordinatorID references Employee:PersonID? Wait, the Department table has CoordinatorID as a foreign key. But the Employee table's primary key is PersonID. So Department.CoordinatorID should reference Employee.PersonID. But in the schema, the Employee table's primary key is PersonID, which is also the primary key of the Person table. So that's okay. But wait, the Coordinator is an Employee, so the CoordinatorID in Department should reference Employee.PersonID. But the Employee table's primary key is PersonID, which is a foreign key from Person. So Department.CoordinatorID is a foreign key to Employee.PersonID. That's valid.

3. Person:PersonID is the primary key for Patient, Visitor, Employee. So their foreign keys are all referencing Person.PersonID. That's correct.

4. Address:PersonID references Person.PersonID – valid.

5. IdentificationCard:PersonID references Employee.PersonID. Wait, the IdentificationCard is linked to Employee, so the PersonID in IdentificationCard should reference Employee.PersonID. But Employee.PersonID is a foreign key to Person.PersonID. So IdentificationCard.PersonID references Employee.PersonID, which is okay because Employee.PersonID is a foreign key to Person. So that's valid.

6. WorkShift:EmployeeID references Employee.PersonID – valid.

7. TimeClockRecord:EmployeeID references Employee.PersonID – valid.

8. AvailabilityException:EmployeeID references Employee.PersonID – valid.

9. ShiftSwap:Employee1ID and Employee2ID reference Employee.PersonID – valid.

10. PatientMovement:PatientID references Patient.PersonID, which in turn references Person.PersonID. So PatientMovement.PatientID references Patient.PersonID, which is a foreign key to Person. That's okay.

11. DepartmentCapacityAlert:DepartmentID references Department.DepartmentID – valid.

Wait, but looking at the relations array, the relations are listed as:

- Department:DepartmentID 1--1 Employee:PersonID (This seems off. The Department has a CoordinatorID which is a foreign key to Employee.PersonID. But the relation here is written as Department:DepartmentID 1--1 Employee:PersonID. That doesn't make sense. The foreign key in Department is CoordinatorID, not DepartmentID. So this relation is incorrect. The correct foreign key should be Department.CoordinatorID referencing Employee.PersonID. So this is an error.)

Another issue is in the relations array, the relation between Department and Employee is written as Department:DepartmentID 1--1 Employee:PersonID. That's incorrect because the foreign key in Department is CoordinatorID, not DepartmentID. So this is an error.

Also, the relation between Employee and ShiftSwap is written as Employee:PersonID 1--* ShiftSwap:Employee1ID and Employee:PersonID 1--* ShiftSwap:Employee2ID. But each ShiftSwap has two Employee IDs, so the foreign keys are Employee1ID and Employee2ID. However, the relation is written as 1--*, which might not capture the two-way relationship correctly. But maybe that's okay if each ShiftSwap can have two employees. However, the way it's written in the relations array is a bit ambiguous. But the main issue is the Department and Coordinator relationship.

So, the foreign key validation has an error in the Department.CoordinatorID referencing Employee.PersonID, but the relation is incorrectly written as Department:DepartmentID 1--1 Employee:PersonID. That's a mistake. The correct foreign key is CoordinatorID in Department referencing Employee.PersonID.

Another possible error is in the Patient table. The Patient table has *PersonID as the primary key, which is a foreign key to Person.PersonID. That's correct. Similarly for Visitor and Employee.

Now, moving to RELATIONSHIP CARDINALITY VALIDATION. Let's check each relation:

1. Hospital:HospitalID 1--* Department:HospitalID – correct, one hospital has many departments.

2. Department:DepartmentID 1--1 Employee:PersonID – this is incorrect. The Department has a CoordinatorID (foreign key to Employee.PersonID), so the correct cardinality should be Department:DepartmentID 1--* Employee:PersonID? Wait, no. Each Department has one Coordinator, so it's 1--1. But the foreign key is CoordinatorID in Department referencing Employee.PersonID. So the correct relation should be Department:CoordinatorID 1--1 Employee:PersonID. However, the current relation is written as Department:DepartmentID 1--1 Employee:PersonID, which is wrong because the foreign key is CoordinatorID, not DepartmentID. So this is an error.

3. Person:PersonID 1--1 Patient:PersonID – correct, each person is a patient once.

4. Person:PersonID 1--1 Visitor:PersonID – correct.

5. Person:PersonID 1--1 Employee:PersonID – correct.

6. Person:PersonID 1--* Address:PersonID – correct, a person can have multiple addresses.

7. Employee:PersonID 1--* IdentificationCard:PersonID – correct, an employee can have multiple cards.

8. Employee:PersonID 1--* WorkShift:EmployeeID – correct.

9. Employee:PersonID 1--* TimeClockRecord:EmployeeID – correct.

10. Employee:PersonID 1--* AvailabilityException:EmployeeID – correct.

11. Employee:PersonID 1--* ShiftSwap:Employee1ID and Employee2ID – each ShiftSwap has two employees, so the relation should be Employee:PersonID *--* ShiftSwap:Employee1ID and Employee2ID. But the current relations are written as 1--*, which is incorrect. Because each ShiftSwap can involve two different employees, the cardinality should be many-to-many, but since ShiftSwap is a junction table, it's okay. However, the way it's written in the relations array is as two separate 1--* relations, which is incorrect. The correct cardinality for ShiftSwap would be Employee:PersonID *--* ShiftSwap:Employee1ID and Employee2ID. But since each ShiftSwap has two employees, the relations should be written as Employee:PersonID *--* ShiftSwap:Employee1ID and Employee:PersonID *--* ShiftSwap:Employee2ID. However, the current relations are written as 1--*, which is incorrect. So this is another error.

12. Patient:PersonID 1--* PatientMovement:PatientID – correct.

13. Department:DepartmentID 1--* PatientMovement:DepartmentID – correct.

14. Department:DepartmentID 1--* DepartmentCapacityAlert:DepartmentID – correct.

So the main cardinality errors are in the Department and Coordinator relationship, and the ShiftSwap relations.

Next, ENTITY COMPLETENESS. The original requirement mentions entities like Hospital, Department, Person, Patient, Visitor, Employee, Address, IdentificationCard, WorkShift, TimeClockRecord, AvailabilityException, ShiftSwap, PatientMovement, DepartmentCapacityAlert. The schema includes all these tables. So the answer is YES.

ATTRIBUTE COMPLETENESS & DATA TYPES. Let's check each table:

- Hospital: Has HospitalID, Name, State, Address. The requirement mentions hospitals have departments, but the Address is stored here. However, the requirement says that addresses for people are stored in the Address table. The Hospital's Address is part of the Hospital table, which is okay. Data types seem appropriate.

- Department: Has DepartmentID, Name, HospitalID, CoordinatorID. The requirement says each department has a coordinator. The CoordinatorID is a foreign key to Employee.PersonID. The data types are correct.

- Person: Has PersonID, FullName, CPF, RG, DOB. The requirement mentions these attributes. Correct.

- Patient: Has PersonID (FK), WristbandID. The requirement says each patient has a wristband. Correct.

- Visitor: Has PersonID (FK), VisitorType, CompanionStatus, IsolationReason. The requirement mentions VisitorType, CompanionStatus, and IsolationReason. Correct.

- Employee: Has PersonID (FK), Role, ContractType. The requirement mentions Role and ContractType. Correct.

- Address: Has AddressID, PersonID (FK), AddressDetails, LastUpdated. The requirement mentions multiple addresses, last update timestamp. Correct.

- IdentificationCard: Has CardID, PersonID (FK), IssueDate, ExpiryDate, CardType, ActiveStatus. The requirement mentions these attributes. Correct.

- WorkShift: Has ShiftID, EmployeeID (FK), StartDateTime, EndDateTime. Correct.

- TimeClockRecord: Has RecordID, EmployeeID (FK), ClockIn, ClockOut. Correct.

- AvailabilityException: Has ExceptionID, EmployeeID (FK), StartDate, EndDate, ExceptionType, Notes. Correct.

- ShiftSwap: Has SwapID, Employee1ID, Employee2ID, CoordinatorID, SwapDate, Justification. The requirement mentions ShiftSwap with two employees and a coordinator. Correct.

- PatientMovement: Has MovementID, PatientID (FK), DepartmentID (FK), Timestamp, Status. Correct.

- DepartmentCapacityAlert: Has AlertID, DepartmentID (FK), AlertLevel, Timestamp. Correct.

All required attributes are present with appropriate data types. So the answer is YES.

MANY-TO-MANY RELATIONSHIPS. The only potential many-to-many relationship is between Employee and ShiftSwap, since each ShiftSwap involves two employees. However, ShiftSwap is a junction table with two foreign keys to Employee. So the many-to-many relationship is properly handled with an intermediary table. So the answer is YES.

Now, compiling the errors found:

1. The relation between Department and Employee is incorrectly written as Department:DepartmentID 1--1 Employee:PersonID. The correct foreign key is Department.CoordinatorID referencing Employee.PersonID. The cardinality should be 1--1, but the relation is written with the wrong attribute.

2. The relations between Employee and ShiftSwap are written as 1--*, but since each ShiftSwap involves two employees, the correct cardinality should be *--* for each foreign key. However, since ShiftSwap is a junction table, it's okay, but the way it's written in the relations array is incorrect. The relations should be Employee:PersonID *--* ShiftSwap:Employee1ID and Employee:PersonID *--* ShiftSwap:Employee2ID.

3. The Department table's CoordinatorID is a foreign key to Employee.PersonID, but the relation is not correctly represented in the relations array. The current relation is written as Department:DepartmentID 1--1 Employee:PersonID, which is incorrect. It should be Department:CoordinatorID 1--1 Employee:PersonID.

These are the main errors. Each of these affects the correctness of the schema by either incorrect foreign key references or incorrect cardinalities.
</think>

VERIFICATION SUMMARY:
1. PRIMARY KEY VALIDATION: YES  
2. FOREIGN KEY VALIDATION: NO  
3. RELATIONSHIP CARDINALITY VALIDATION: NO  
4. ENTITY COMPLETENESS: YES  
5. ATTRIBUTE COMPLETENESS & DATA TYPES: YES  
6. MANY-TO-MANY RELATIONSHIPS: YES  

ERRORS FOUND: 3  
ERROR #1:  
  - Issue: Department table's CoordinatorID foreign key is incorrectly represented in the relations array as `Department:DepartmentID 1--1 Employee:PersonID` instead of `Department:CoordinatorID 1--1 Employee:PersonID`.  
  - Impact: Misrepresents the relationship between departments and their coordinators, leading to potential data integrity issues.  
  - Fix: Correct the relation to reference `Department.CoordinatorID` instead of `Department.DepartmentID`.  

ERROR #2:  
  - Issue: ShiftSwap relations are incorrectly defined as `Employee:PersonID 1--* ShiftSwap:Employee1ID` and `Employee:PersonID 1--* ShiftSwap:Employee2ID`. Each ShiftSwap involves two employees, requiring a many-to-many relationship.  
  - Impact: Incorrect cardinality may lead to invalid constraints or data anomalies when recording shift swaps.  
  - Fix: Update relations to `Employee:PersonID *--* ShiftSwap:Employee1ID` and `Employee:PersonID *--* ShiftSwap:Employee2ID` to reflect the many-to-many nature.  

ERROR #3:  
  - Issue: The Department table's CoordinatorID foreign key (referencing Employee.PersonID) is not explicitly validated in the schema. While the foreign key exists, the relation in the "relations" array is misaligned.  
  - Impact: Risks orphaned records if a department's coordinator is deleted without updating the department.  
  - Fix: Ensure the `Department.CoordinatorID` foreign key is explicitly validated as referencing `Employee.PersonID` in the relations array.  

CONFIDENCE: Medium  
  Reasoning: The schema is mostly correct but contains critical errors in foreign key and cardinality definitions that could compromise data integrity. Addressing these issues will ensure compliance with the requirements.

formatted_human_prompt:
Here is a schema that had validation errors:
    {'tables': {'Hospital': {'*HospitalID': 'int', 'Name': 'varchar(100)', 'State': 'varchar(50)', 'Address': 'varchar(200)'}, 'Department': {'*DepartmentID': 'int', 'Name': 'varchar(50)', 'HospitalID': 'int', 'CoordinatorID': 'int'}, 'Person': {'*PersonID': 'int', 'FullName': 'varchar(100)', 'CPF': 'varchar(14)', 'RG': 'varchar(20)', 'DOB': 'date'}, 'Patient': {'*PersonID': 'int', 'WristbandID': 'varchar(20)'}, 'Visitor': {'*PersonID': 'int', 'VisitorType': 'varchar(50)', 'CompanionStatus': 'varchar(50)', 'IsolationReason': 'text'}, 'Employee': {'*PersonID': 'int', 'Role': 'varchar(50)', 'ContractType': 'varchar(50)'}, 'Address': {'*AddressID': 'int', 'PersonID': 'int', 'AddressDetails': 'text', 'LastUpdated': 'timestamp'}, 'IdentificationCard': {'*CardID': 'int', 'PersonID': 'int', 'IssueDate': 'date', 'ExpiryDate': 'date', 'CardType': 'varchar(50)', 'ActiveStatus': 'boolean'}, 'WorkShift': {'*ShiftID': 'int', 'EmployeeID': 'int', 'StartDateTime': 'datetime', 'EndDateTime': 'datetime'}, 'TimeClockRecord': {'*RecordID': 'int', 'EmployeeID': 'int', 'ClockIn': 'datetime', 'ClockOut': 'datetime'}, 'AvailabilityException': {'*ExceptionID': 'int', 'EmployeeID': 'int', 'StartDate': 'date', 'EndDate': 'date', 'ExceptionType': 'varchar(50)', 'Notes': 'text'}, 'ShiftSwap': {'*SwapID': 'int', 'Employee1ID': 'int', 'Employee2ID': 'int', 'CoordinatorID': 'int', 'SwapDate': 'date', 'Justification': 'text'}, 'PatientMovement': {'*MovementID': 'int', 'PatientID': 'int', 'DepartmentID': 'int', 'Timestamp': 'datetime', 'Status': 'varchar(50)'}, 'DepartmentCapacityAlert': {'*AlertID': 'int', 'DepartmentID': 'int', 'AlertLevel': 'varchar(10)', 'Timestamp': 'datetime'}}, 'relations': ['Hospital:HospitalID 1--* Department:HospitalID', 'Department:DepartmentID 1--1 Employee:PersonID', 'Person:PersonID 1--1 Patient:PersonID', 'Person:PersonID 1--1 Visitor:PersonID', 'Person:PersonID 1--1 Employee:PersonID', 'Person:PersonID 1--* Address:PersonID', 'Employee:PersonID 1--* IdentificationCard:PersonID', 'Employee:PersonID 1--* WorkShift:EmployeeID', 'Employee:PersonID 1--* TimeClockRecord:EmployeeID', 'Employee:PersonID 1--* AvailabilityException:EmployeeID', 'Employee:PersonID 1--* ShiftSwap:Employee1ID', 'Employee:PersonID 1--* ShiftSwap:Employee2ID', 'Patient:PersonID 1--* PatientMovement:PatientID', 'Department:DepartmentID 1--* PatientMovement:DepartmentID', 'Department:DepartmentID 1--* DepartmentCapacityAlert:DepartmentID'], 'rankAdjustments': '', 'label': 'Integrated Hospital Management System ER Model'}

    ERRORS FOUND:
    ERRORS FOUND: 3  
ERROR #1:  
  - Issue: Department table's CoordinatorID foreign key is incorrectly represented in the relations array as `Department:DepartmentID 1--1 Employee:PersonID` instead of `Department:CoordinatorID 1--1 Employee:PersonID`.  
  - Impact: Misrepresents the relationship between departments and their coordinators, leading to potential data integrity issues.  
  - Fix: Correct the relation to reference `Department.CoordinatorID` instead of `Department.DepartmentID`.  

ERROR #2:  
  - Issue: ShiftSwap relations are incorrectly defined as `Employee:PersonID 1--* ShiftSwap:Employee1ID` and `Employee:PersonID 1--* ShiftSwap:Employee2ID`. Each ShiftSwap involves two employees, requiring a many-to-many relationship.  
  - Impact: Incorrect cardinality may lead to invalid constraints or data anomalies when recording shift swaps.  
  - Fix: Update relations to `Employee:PersonID *--* ShiftSwap:Employee1ID` and `Employee:PersonID *--* ShiftSwap:Employee2ID` to reflect the many-to-many nature.  

ERROR #3:  
  - Issue: The Department table's CoordinatorID foreign key (referencing Employee.PersonID) is not explicitly validated in the schema. While the foreign key exists, the relation in the "relations" array is misaligned.  
  - Impact: Risks orphaned records if a department's coordinator is deleted without updating the department.  
  - Fix: Ensure the `Department.CoordinatorID` foreign key is explicitly validated as referencing `Employee.PersonID` in the relations array.  

CONFIDENCE: Medium  
  Reasoning: The schema is mostly correct but contains critical errors in foreign key and cardinality definitions that could compromise data integrity. Addressing these issues will ensure compliance with the requirements.

    REQUIREMENT:
    ER Modeling Scenario
Integrated Hospital Management System for Multiple Facilities
Business Context
You have been hired to model the database for a hospital network that operates 15 units across 3 Brazilian states. The system must control, record and monitor the entry and exit of all people who access the hospitals, including staff, visitors and patients, ensuring traceability, security and compliance with the institution's internal rules.
Functional Requirements
1. Hospital
Each hospital has departments responsible for specific activities. Not every facility in the network needs to offer all services: each hospital declares the departments, human resources and capacities it has (available teams and departments).
Departments present in the network:
* Emergency
* ICU (Intensive Care Unit)
* Surgical Center / Operating Theaters
* Adult Ward
* Pediatric Ward
* Maternity
* Radiology / Imaging (X-ray, CT, MRI)
* Clinical Laboratory
* Outpatient Clinic
* Hospital Pharmacy
* Social Services
* Triage
* Reception
1.1 Departments
* Each department has an assigned coordinator.
* There must be a leader or person responsible for the department in every shift (there should always be someone accountable for the department at any time).

2. People Management
The hospital receives individuals in multiple categories; some are employees and others are not. Categories include: visitor, patient, physician, nurse, resident physician, researcher and administrative staff. Physicians may work in multiple units under different contract types (for example, CLT, PJ, on-call, daily-rate). Residents are licensed physicians in training who also attend patients under supervision, so it is important to record the supervising physician for each resident. VIP patients have special care rules and enhanced privacy (these are procedural and privacy measures, not related to the medical quality of care). Employees may have dependents who are also patients. The hospital records the following for every person who accesses it: full name, addresses, contact phone numbers, CPF, RG and date of birth. (CPF and RG are Brazilian identifiers: CPF = taxpayer ID, RG = identity card.)
2.1 Patients
   * Each patient receives an identification wristband.
   * The system must record patient movements between departments, including hospital admission, transfers between departments and discharge.
   * A complete history of the departments each patient has passed through must be maintained.
   * The system must indicate whether the patient is awaiting care, in care, admitted (inpatient), under observation, in surgery, or discharged.
2.2 Visitors
   * Not every visitor is accompanying a patient.
   * The type of visitor must be recorded (for example, technical visit, regulatory inspection, etc.).
   * Visitor classification is essential to determine access restrictions (important information for the security team).
   * Each visitor receives an identification card at entry.
   * Not every patient is allowed to have a companion. Valid companion cases include: minors; elderly patients; pregnant patients; women in labor; postpartum patients; patients with prolonged or severe hospitalization, at the discretion of the attending physician or care team; and situations of emotional or cognitive vulnerability when the physician or social services deem it necessary.
   * If patient isolation is required, the physician must document the reason for isolation in a clinical note.
   * The system must record the visitor's exit (mandatory).
   * Access outside visiting hours must be prevented unless special authorization is granted by the hospital.
2.3 Employees
   * Employees must be clearly identified.
   * A researcher can be either a nurse or a physician only; researcher is not an independent occupational category.
   * A resident physician cannot be a coordinator or be responsible for a department, even temporarily.
   * Physicians and nurses may also hold administrative roles (for example, responsible for coordinating a department).
   * Any employee may still be treated by the hospital, be present as a visitor, or accompany a patient.
2.4 Addresses
   * A person may have more than one registered address.
   * Employees update their addresses periodically.
   * When possible, patients and visitors should also have updated addresses.
   * It is important to store the timestamp of the last address update.
   * If the last update was more than six months ago, the system should alert and request a registration update.
2.5 Identification Cards
   * Identification cards have an issue date and an expiry date, recorded in the system for security purposes.
   * Cards have a type that designates access level (visually distinguished by different colors).
   * When a card is in use it is marked as active.
   * Because patients and visitors do not have personalized cards, a card's identifier number may be temporarily linked to the person's record while in use.
   * Only personalized cards (employees) must include a photo.
3. Employee Scheduling
Hospital units require an integrated time and attendance system that covers all 15 units to prevent staff from being accidentally scheduled at two locations at the same time or assigned impractical schedules. Professionals follow different work routines. Administration must precisely control the hours worked by each employee. Different hospitals in the network may have different scheduling contracts with the same employee. It is also necessary to record events that remove an employee from duties, such as days off, medical leave, vacation or training.
3.1 Time Clock Records
   * The system must record each employee's clock-in and clock-out times.
   * As mentioned, each employee has an identification card, which facilitates this task.
   * Time records may generate an alert if an employee exceeds scheduled hours or misses a clock record; this is useful for HR.
3.2 Work Shifts
   * Some employees have more flexible shifts than others.
   * Administrative staff generally have fixed weekdays and start/end times.
   * Nurses may have fixed rules or work rotating shifts.
   * Physicians similarly may have variable schedules and may come to the hospital only to perform consultations, monitoring, surgeries, etc., leaving when those activities are completed.
   * The employment contract governs how working hours are administered by the contracting institution.
   * The key requirement is accurate recording of clock-in and clock-out times for HR verification of compliance with agreed schedules.
3.3 Availability Exceptions
   * Reasons that remove a professional from scheduled activities must be well documented.
   * Each exception should include start and end dates, type (day off, medical leave, vacation, training) and an optional textual note.
3.4 Shift Swaps
   * Shift swaps among health staff are common and permitted.
   * Whenever a swap occurs, it is important to record the involved employees and the coordinator who approved the swap.
   * Each department has its coordinator, who may be a physician, a nurse or an administrative employee.
   * The justification for the swap request should also be recorded (useful for HR metrics about the need for permanent schedule changes, employee health considerations, etc.).
4. Special Situations and Contingency Procedures
4.1 Lost Identification Card
The system must allow immediate registration of a lost card. When a loss is reported:
   * the card is automatically invalidated;
   * a new card may be issued and linked to the person;
   * every access attempt using the lost card is blocked and logged;
   * the event generates an alert for security.
A history of cards (active, replaced, lost, expired) must be maintained.
4.2 Card Failure (Defective or Inoperable Card)
When a card fails to read, the system must record: the person involved; the reason for the failure; the read attempt; and the manual verification performed (security or reception staff responsible). Manual validation of entry/exit using an official document plus employee ID/registration must be allowed. After manual validation, the system records the entry/exit with the same legal validity as an electronic read. The defective card should be marked inoperable and require replacement.
5. Reports and Queries
      * Query access history by person, date, department and type (employee, visitor, patient).
      * Generate department occupancy reports.
      * Generate reports on compliance with scheduled hours and total hours worked for a given period (month, week, day).
      * The system must alert whenever a department reaches 60%, 80% and 100% of its operational capacity.

    Generate a CORRECTED schema that fixes all identified errors.
    Use the 5-step reasoning process from Level 1.
    Output only the corrected JSON at the end.

initial_schema.label: Integrated Hospital Management System ER Model

format_params_used.errors_summary:
ERRORS FOUND: 3  
ERROR #1:  
  - Issue: Department table's CoordinatorID foreign key is incorrectly represented in the relations array as `Department:DepartmentID 1--1 Employee:PersonID` instead of `Department:CoordinatorID 1--1 Employee:PersonID`.  
  - Impact: Misrepresents the relationship between departments and their coordinators, leading to potential data integrity issues.  
  - Fix: Correct the relation to reference `Department.CoordinatorID` instead of `Department.DepartmentID`.  

ERROR #2:  
  - Issue: ShiftSwap relations are incorrectly defined as `Employee:PersonID 1--* ShiftSwap:Employee1ID` and `Employee:PersonID 1--* ShiftSwap:Employee2ID`. Each ShiftSwap involves two employees, requiring a many-to-many relationship.  
  - Impact: Incorrect cardinality may lead to invalid constraints or data anomalies when recording shift swaps.  
  - Fix: Update relations to `Employee:PersonID *--* ShiftSwap:Employee1ID` and `Employee:PersonID *--* ShiftSwap:Employee2ID` to reflect the many-to-many nature.  

ERROR #3:  
  - Issue: The Department table's CoordinatorID foreign key (referencing Employee.PersonID) is not explicitly validated in the schema. While the foreign key exists, the relation in the "relations" array is misaligned.  
  - Impact: Risks orphaned records if a department's coordinator is deleted without updating the department.  
  - Fix: Ensure the `Department.CoordinatorID` foreign key is explicitly validated as referencing `Employee.PersonID` in the relations array.  

CONFIDENCE: Medium  
  Reasoning: The schema is mostly correct but contains critical errors in foreign key and cardinality definitions that could compromise data integrity. Addressing these issues will ensure compliance with the requirements.

response:
<think>
Okay, let's tackle this problem step by step. The user provided a schema with some errors and wants a corrected version. First, I need to understand the requirements and the errors mentioned.

Starting with the entities. The original schema has Hospital, Department, Person, Patient, Visitor, Employee, Address, IdentificationCard, WorkShift, TimeClockRecord, AvailabilityException, ShiftSwap, PatientMovement, DepartmentCapacityAlert. That seems comprehensive. 

Now, looking at the attributes. The main issue is with the Department's CoordinatorID. The error says that the relation was incorrectly pointing to DepartmentID instead of CoordinatorID. So I need to adjust the relations array to reference CoordinatorID correctly. Also, the ShiftSwap relations were defined as 1--* but should be *--* because each swap involves two employees. 

For the Department table, the CoordinatorID is a foreign key to Employee.PersonID. So the relation should be Department:CoordinatorID 1--1 Employee:PersonID. Wait, the error mentioned that the original relation was Department:DepartmentID 1--1 Employee:PersonID, which is wrong. So correcting that to use CoordinatorID.

Then, the ShiftSwap has two foreign keys: Employee1ID and Employee2ID. The original relations were Employee:PersonID 1--* ShiftSwap:Employee1ID and similarly for Employee2ID. But since each ShiftSwap involves two employees, the cardinality should be many-to-many. So the relations should be Employee:PersonID *--* ShiftSwap:Employee1ID and the same for Employee2ID. 

Also, the error #3 mentions that the CoordinatorID foreign key isn't explicitly validated. So in the relations array, we need to add the Department:CoordinatorID 1--1 Employee:PersonID to ensure that the foreign key is properly referenced.

Now, checking the other relations. For example, the Department has a HospitalID foreign key, which is correctly referenced. The Person table is the parent for Patient, Visitor, Employee, which is correct. Each of these has a 1--1 relation with Person. 

The Address table has a PersonID foreign key, which is 1--* because a person can have multiple addresses. That seems okay. 

The IdentificationCard has a PersonID foreign key, which is 1--* since an employee can have multiple cards over time. 

WorkShift, TimeClockRecord, AvailabilityException all have EmployeeID foreign keys pointing to Employee.PersonID. Those relations are 1--* as each employee can have multiple shifts, etc. 

ShiftSwap's relations were the main issue. The original relations had 1--* but should be *--* because each ShiftSwap can involve any two employees, and each employee can be in multiple swaps. So changing those to *--*.

Now, validating the corrected schema. Let's check each table's primary keys and foreign keys. 

Department's CoordinatorID should be a foreign key to Employee.PersonID. The original schema had it as an int, which is correct. The relations array now includes Department:CoordinatorID 1--1 Employee:PersonID. 

ShiftSwap's relations are now Employee:PersonID *--* ShiftSwap:Employee1ID and similarly for Employee2ID. 

Also, ensuring that all foreign keys in the relations array correctly reference the right columns. For example, Department:HospitalID 1--* Hospital:HospitalID is correct. 

Now, checking for any other possible issues. The Address table's PersonID is a foreign key to Person.PersonID, which is correct. The IdentificationCard's PersonID is a foreign key to Employee.PersonID, which is okay since only employees have identification cards. 

The PatientMovement has PatientID and DepartmentID as foreign keys, which are correctly referenced. 

DepartmentCapacityAlert's DepartmentID is correctly referenced. 

Now, making sure that all the relations in the relations array are correctly formatted and that the cardinalities are accurate. For example, the ShiftSwap relations are now *--*, which reflects that many employees can be involved in many swaps through their respective Employee1ID and Employee2ID. 

Also, the Department's CoordinatorID relation is 1--1 because each department has exactly one coordinator, and each coordinator can be the coordinator of one department at a time? Wait, the requirement says that a resident physician cannot be a coordinator, but there's no restriction on how many departments an employee can coordinate. Wait, the original error didn't mention that. The error was about the foreign key reference. So the cardinality for Department:CoordinatorID 1--1 Employee:PersonID might be incorrect if an employee can be a coordinator for multiple departments. 

Wait, the requirement says that each department has an assigned coordinator. So a department has one coordinator, but an employee can be a coordinator for multiple departments. Therefore, the cardinality should be Department:CoordinatorID 1--* Employee:PersonID. Because one employee can be the coordinator for multiple departments, but each department has exactly one coordinator. 

Wait, the original error mentioned that the relation was Department:DepartmentID 1--1 Employee:PersonID, which is wrong. The correct relation is Department:CoordinatorID 1--* Employee:PersonID. Because each department has one coordinator (1), but an employee can be a coordinator for multiple departments (*). 

So the cardinality should be 1--*, not 1--1. The original error's fix suggested changing to Department:CoordinatorID 1--1 Employee:PersonID, but that would imply that an employee can only be a coordinator for one department, which may not be the case. 

Wait, the user's error description says: "Fix: Correct the relation to reference Department.CoordinatorID instead of Department.DepartmentID." But the cardinality was 1--1. However, based on the business rules, a department has one coordinator, but an employee can be a coordinator for multiple departments. Therefore, the correct cardinality is Department:CoordinatorID 1--* Employee:PersonID. 

So the original error's fix might have been incomplete. The user's error description says that the relation was misaligned, but the correct cardinality is 1--*. 

So in the corrected schema, the Department:CoordinatorID relation should be 1--* Employee:PersonID. 

Similarly, for ShiftSwap, the relations are *--* because each employee can be involved in multiple swaps, and each swap involves two employees. 

So adjusting the relations accordingly. 

Now, updating the relations array:

- Department:HospitalID 1--* Hospital:HospitalID
- Department:DepartmentID 1--* PatientMovement:DepartmentID
- Department:DepartmentID 1--* DepartmentCapacityAlert:DepartmentID
- Department:CoordinatorID 1--* Employee:PersonID
- Person:PersonID 1--1 Patient:PersonID
- Person:PersonID 1--1 Visitor:PersonID
- Person:PersonID 1--1 Employee:PersonID
- Person:PersonID 1--* Address:PersonID
- Employee:PersonID 1--* IdentificationCard:PersonID
- Employee:PersonID 1--* WorkShift:EmployeeID
- Employee:PersonID 1--* TimeClockRecord:EmployeeID
- Employee:PersonID 1--* AvailabilityException:EmployeeID
- Employee:PersonID *--* ShiftSwap:Employee1ID
- Employee:PersonID *--* ShiftSwap:Employee2ID
- Patient:PersonID 1--* PatientMovement:PatientID

Also, the Department:DepartmentID 1--1 Employee:PersonID was incorrect. That relation was for the CoordinatorID, which is now corrected to Department:CoordinatorID 1--* Employee:PersonID.

So the corrected relations array should include:

- Hospital:HospitalID 1--* Department:HospitalID
- Department:CoordinatorID 1--* Employee:PersonID
- Person:PersonID 1--1 Patient:PersonID
- Person:PersonID 1--1 Visitor:PersonID
- Person:PersonID 1--1 Employee:PersonID
- Person:PersonID 1--* Address:PersonID
- Employee:PersonID 1--* IdentificationCard:PersonID
- Employee:PersonID 1--* WorkShift:EmployeeID
- Employee:PersonID 1--* TimeClockRecord:EmployeeID
- Employee:PersonID 1--* AvailabilityException:EmployeeID
- Employee:PersonID *--* ShiftSwap:Employee1ID
- Employee:PersonID *--* ShiftSwap:Employee2ID
- Patient:PersonID 1--* PatientMovement:PatientID
- Department:DepartmentID 1--* PatientMovement:DepartmentID
- Department:DepartmentID 1--* DepartmentCapacityAlert:DepartmentID

This should address all the errors mentioned. Now, checking each relation:

- Department's CoordinatorID is a foreign key to Employee's PersonID, with cardinality 1--*, which allows an employee to be a coordinator for multiple departments.
- ShiftSwap's Employee1ID and Employee2ID have *--* cardinality, allowing any number of swaps between any number of employees.
- All other relations seem correctly defined based on the requirements.

Now, ensuring that all foreign keys in the tables are correctly referenced. For example, Department's CoordinatorID references Employee.PersonID, which is correct. ShiftSwap's Employee1ID and Employee2ID reference Employee.PersonID, which is correct.

Finally, the label should be "Integrated Hospital Management System ER Model" as before, and rankAdjustments remains an empty string.
</think>

REASONING:
STEP 1 - ENTITIES: [Hospital, Department, Person, Patient, Visitor, Employee, Address, IdentificationCard, WorkShift, TimeClockRecord, AvailabilityException, ShiftSwap, PatientMovement, DepartmentCapacityAlert]

STEP 2 - ATTRIBUTES: 
ENTITY Hospital:
  *HospitalID: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  Name: varchar(100)
  State: varchar(50)
  Address: varchar(200)

ENTITY Department:
  *DepartmentID: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  Name: varchar(50)
  HospitalID: int
  CoordinatorID: int

ENTITY Person:
  *PersonID: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  FullName: varchar(100)
  CPF: varchar(14)
  RG: varchar(20)
  DOB: date

ENTITY Patient:
  *PersonID: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  WristbandID: varchar(20)

ENTITY Visitor:
  *PersonID: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  VisitorType: varchar(50)
  CompanionStatus: varchar(50)
  IsolationReason: text

ENTITY Employee:
  *PersonID: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  Role: varchar(50)
  ContractType: varchar(50)

ENTITY Address:
  *AddressID: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  PersonID: int
  AddressDetails: text
  LastUpdated: timestamp

ENTITY IdentificationCard:
  *CardID: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  PersonID: int
  IssueDate: date
  ExpiryDate: date
  CardType: varchar(50)
  ActiveStatus: boolean

ENTITY WorkShift:
  *ShiftID: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  EmployeeID: int
  StartDateTime: datetime
  EndDateTime: datetime

ENTITY TimeClockRecord:
  *RecordID: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  EmployeeID: int
  ClockIn: datetime
  ClockOut: datetime

ENTITY AvailabilityException:
  *ExceptionID: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  EmployeeID: int
  StartDate: date
  EndDate: date
  ExceptionType: varchar(50)
  Notes: text

ENTITY ShiftSwap:
  *SwapID: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  Employee1ID: int
  Employee2ID: int
  CoordinatorID: int
  SwapDate: date
  Justification: text

ENTITY PatientMovement:
  *MovementID: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  PatientID: int
  DepartmentID: int
  Timestamp: datetime
  Status: varchar(50)

ENTITY DepartmentCapacityAlert:
  *AlertID: int (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)
  DepartmentID: int
  AlertLevel: varchar(10)
  Timestamp: datetime

STEP 3 - RELATIONSHIPS: 
RELATIONSHIPS:
  - Hospital has many Departments (Hospital-HospitalID to Department-HospitalID)
  - Department has one Coordinator (Department-CoordinatorID to Employee-PersonID)
  - Person is either Patient/Visitor/Employee (Person-PersonID to Patient/Visitor/Employee-PersonID)
  - Person has multiple Addresses (Person-PersonID to Address-PersonID)
  - Employee has multiple IdentificationCards (Employee-PersonID to IdentificationCard-PersonID)
  - Employee has multiple WorkShifts (Employee-PersonID to WorkShift-EmployeeID)
  - Employee has multiple TimeClockRecords (Employee-PersonID to TimeClockRecord-EmployeeID)
  - Employee has multiple AvailabilityExceptions (Employee-PersonID to AvailabilityException-EmployeeID)
  - Employee can be involved in multiple ShiftSwaps (Employee-PersonID to ShiftSwap-Employee1ID/Employee2ID)
  - Patient has multiple PatientMovements (Patient-PersonID to PatientMovement-PatientID)
  - Department has multiple PatientMovements (Department-DepartmentID to PatientMovement-DepartmentID)
  - Department has multiple CapacityAlerts (Department-DepartmentID to DepartmentCapacityAlert-DepartmentID)

STEP 4 - CARDINALITIES & FK: 
CARDINALITY & FK:
  Relationship 1: Hospital:HospitalID 1--* Department:HospitalID
  Relationship 2: Department:CoordinatorID 1--* Employee:PersonID
  Relationship 3: Person:PersonID 1--1 Patient:PersonID
  Relationship 4: Person:PersonID 1--1 Visitor:PersonID
  Relationship 5: Person:PersonID 1--1 Employee:PersonID
  Relationship 6: Person:PersonID 1--* Address:PersonID
  Relationship 7: Employee:PersonID 1--* IdentificationCard:PersonID
  Relationship 8: Employee:PersonID 1--* WorkShift:EmployeeID
  Relationship 9: Employee:PersonID 1--* TimeClockRecord:EmployeeID
  Relationship 10: Employee:PersonID 1--* AvailabilityException:EmployeeID
  Relationship 11: Employee:PersonID *--* ShiftSwap:Employee1ID
  Relationship 12: Employee:PersonID *--* ShiftSwap:Employee2ID
  Relationship 13: Patient:PersonID 1--* PatientMovement:PatientID
  Relationship 14: Department:DepartmentID 1--* PatientMovement:DepartmentID
  Relationship 15: Department:DepartmentID 1--* DepartmentCapacityAlert:DepartmentID

STEP 5 - VALIDATION: 
VALIDATION:
  ✓ All primary keys defined and unique
  ✓ All foreign keys valid
  ✓ Cardinalities are logical (many-to-many for ShiftSwap, 1-to-many for most relationships)
  ✓ Many-to-many handled with explicit *--* notation
  ✓ No orphaned tables or attributes
  ✓ Fixed Department.CoordinatorID relationship to correctly reference Employee.PersonID
  ✓ Corrected ShiftSwap to use *--* cardinality for both employee relationships
